name: Airavata
description: Airavata, the divine white elephant, is a central figure in Hindu mythology, serving as the mount (vahana) of the god Indra, king of the gods. He emerged from the churning of the Ocean of Milk (Samudra Manthana), a pivotal event in Hindu cosmology. Airavata is described as possessing immense size and strength, often depicted with multiple tusks and a pearly white hide. He is considered the king of all elephants and represents power, prosperity, and rain. His association with Indra signifies his role in bringing rain and fertility to the earth. Airavata's name translates to 'the one who knits or binds the clouds,' further emphasizing his connection to water and abundance. He is believed to reside in Indraloka, Indra's celestial abode. Airavata is not merely a beast of burden; he is a symbol of cosmic order and divine grace, embodying the qualities of royalty and sovereignty. In some accounts, he is said to have four tusks and seven trunks, representing the cardinal directions and the seven seas. He is a guardian deity, protecting Indra and maintaining balance in the universe. His presence in various Hindu scriptures and art underscores his enduring significance as a powerful and auspicious figure in the Hindu pantheon, representing the power and majesty of the divine realm. His connection to the churning of the ocean links him to the origins of the universe and the creation of life.
subtitle: Indra's Divine Elephant Mount
origin: Churning of the Ocean of Milk

keyMyths.title: Emergence from Samudra Manthana
keyMyths.description: Airavata arose from the churning of the Ocean of Milk, becoming Indra's mount and a symbol of prosperity. This event highlights his divine origins and auspicious nature.

keyMyths.title: Association with Rainfall
keyMyths.description: Airavata is closely linked to rain and fertility, reflecting Indra's role as the god of rain and thunder. He symbolizes the abundance and life-giving properties of water.

sources.author: Vettam Mani
